question: What are the fuel requirements for VFR flight in Canada?
ground_truth: For an aircraft other than a helicopter in VFR flight: during the day, enough fuel to fly to the destination plus 30 minutes at normal cruising speed; at night, enough fuel to fly to the destination plus 45 minutes at normal cruising speed. For a helicopter: enough fuel to fly to the destination plus 20 minutes at normal cruising speed.
answer:
For VFR flight in Canada, the fuel requirements are as follows:
1. An amount of fuel that is sufficient to allow the aircraft to fly to the destination aerodrome and then to fly for 30 minutes at normal cruising speed during the day, or 45 minutes at normal cruising speed at night (Section 3.11.1).
2. In addition to the above, the aircraft must carry fuel for taxiing, foreseeable delays prior to takeoff, meteorological conditions, foreseeable air traffic routings and traffic delays, landing at a suitable aerodrome in case of emergencies, and any other foreseeable conditions that could delay the landing of the aircraft (Section 3.12).
3. The pilot should clearly indicate when submitting an arrival report that they were operating on a flight plan or flight itinerary and wish it to be closed. Failure to close a flight plan or flight itinerary will initiate SAR action (Section 3.12).

question: What are the fuel requirements for IFR flight in Canada?
ground_truth: For a propeller-driven aeroplane in IFR flight: enough fuel to fly to the destination, then to the alternate aerodrome, then for 45 minutes at normal cruising speed.
answer:
For IFR flight in Canada, the fuel requirements are as follows:
1. For propeller-driven aeroplanes:
   - Fly to the destination aerodrome, then to the alternate aerodrome (if specified), and then fly for a period of 45 minutes (Section 3.12.2).
2. For turbojet powered aeroplanes or helicopters:
   - Fly to the destination aerodrome, then to the alternate aerodrome (if specified) (Section 3.12.2).
3. The pilot must carry contingency fuel equal to at least 10 percent of the fuel required to complete the flight to the destination aerodrome (Section 12.3).

question: What is the emergency frequency that pilots should monitor?
ground_truth: 121.5 MHz is the emergency frequency.
answer: Pilots should continuously monitor the VHF aeronautical emergency frequency 121.5 MHz as per Section 121.5 and Section 243.0 of the TC AIM.

question: In which airspace classes are transponders required in Canada?
ground_truth: Transponders are required in all Class A, B, and C airspace as well as some Class D and Class E airspace.
answer: Transponders are required in all Class A, B, and C airspace as well as some Class D and E airspace. The requirement for a transponder in Class D and E airspace can be found in the Designated Airspace Handbook (CAR 601.03) and COM subpart 8.2 of the TC AIM. (Section 3.2.34.1, Section 8.2)

question: What is a NOTAM?
ground_truth: A NOTAM is a notice to airman that contains information concerning the establishment or condition of, or any changes in, any aeronautical facility, service, procedure or hazard, the timely knowledge of which is essential to personnel involved in flight operations.
answer: A NOTAM is a notice to airmen that contains information concerning the establishment or condition of, or any changes in, any aeronautical facility, service, procedure, or hazard, the timely knowledge of which is essential to personnel involved in flight operations. NOTAMs are originated and issued promptly whenever the information to be distributed is of a temporary nature and of short duration, or when operationally significant permanent changes or temporary changes of long duration are made at short notice. NOTAMs can be used to advertise changes to the information on aeronautical charts or in aeronautical information publications (Section 3.3.2.2, Section 2.5.4).

question: What are the right-of-way rules when two aircraft are converging?
ground_truth: When two aircraft are converging at approximately the same altitude, the pilot-in-command of the aircraft that has the other on its right shall give way.
answer:
When two aircraft are converging at approximately the same altitude, the pilot-in-command of the aircraft that has the other on its right shall give way, except as follows:
- Section 602.24(5) states that when two aircraft are approaching head-on or approximately so and there is a risk of collision, the pilot-in-command of each aircraft shall alter its heading to the right.
- Section 1.7.3(2) also mentions that when two aircraft are converging at approximately the same altitude, the pilot-in-command of the aircraft that has the other on its right shall give way.

Therefore, in cases of converging aircraft, the pilot-in-command of the aircraft that has the other on its right has the right-of-way.

question: Can aerobatic manoeuvres be performed with passengers on board?
ground_truth: No person operating an aircraft with a passenger on board shall conduct aerobatic manoeuvres unless the pilot-in-command of the aircraft has engaged in at least 10 hours dual flight instruction in aerobatic manoeuvres.
answer: Aerobatic manoeuvres cannot be performed with passengers on board unless the pilot-in-command of the aircraft has met specific requirements outlined in Section 602.28. These requirements include at least 10 hours of dual flight instruction in conducting aerobatic manoeuvres or 20 hours conducting aerobatic manoeuvres, as well as at least one hour of conducting aerobatic manoeuvres in the preceding six months. Additionally, the pilot-in-command must have engaged in aerobatic manoeuvres without passengers on board. (Section 602.28)

question: When is supplemental oxygen required in an unpressurized aircraft?
ground_truth: Supplemental oxygen is required for unpressurized aircraft operating at pressure-altitudes above 13,000 feet ASL.
answer: Supplemental oxygen is required in an unpressurized aircraft for all crew members and 10 percent of passengers, and in any case, no less than one passenger, for the entire period of flight exceeding 30 minutes at cabin-pressure-altitudes above 10,000 feet ASL but not exceeding 13,000 feet ASL, as per Section 605.31.

question: What minimum flight time is required for a Private Pilot Licence (Aeroplane)?
ground_truth: A minimum of 45 hours total flight time is required, including 17 hours dual instruction and 12 hours solo.
answer: For a Private Pilot Licence (Aeroplane), a minimum of 45 hours of flight time is required, including dual instruction of 17 hours and solo flight of 12 hours, as outlined in Section 1.7.2.

question: What medical category is required for a Private Pilot Licence?
ground_truth: A Category 1 or Category 3 medical is required for a Private Pilot Licence.
answer: For a Private Pilot Licence, a medical category of 1 or 3 is required. This information can be found in [Section 1.7.2].

question: What equipment is required for night VFR flight in a power-driven aircraft?
ground_truth: Equipment includes a sensitive altimeter adjustable for barometric pressure, a turn and slip indicator or turn coordinator, an adequate source of electrical energy, spare fuses if applicable, a stabilized magnetic direction indicator or gyroscopic direction indicator, radiocommunication equipment for two-way communication, and position and anti-collision lights.
answer:
For night VFR flight in a power-driven aircraft, the following equipment is required according to the Canadian Aviation Regulations:

1. Altimeter (Section 605.17)
2. Vertical speed indicator (Section 605.17)
3. Power failure warning device or vacuum indicator (Section 605.20)
4. Power available indicator for gyroscopic instruments (Section 605.20)
5. Illumination for all instruments used by the flight crew, including a flashlight (Section 605.20)
6. Alternative source of static pressure for the altimeter, airspeed indicator, and vertical speed indicator (Section 605.20)
7. Radiocommunication equipment for two-way communications on the appropriate frequency (Section 605.20)
8. Radio navigation equipment (Section 605.20)
9. Attitude indicator (Section 605.18)
10. Turn and slip indicator or turn coordinator (Section 605.18)
11. Vertical speed indicator (Section 605.18)
12. Outside air temperature gauge (Section 605.18)

These are the equipment requirements for night VFR flight in a power-driven aircraft as per the provided regulations.

question: What position lights are required on an aircraft at night?
ground_truth: A red light on the port (left) side, a green light on the starboard (right) side, and a white light on the tail.
answer: Traditional aircraft are required to have position lights, which include a red light on the port side (left side when sitting in the pilot’s seat), a green light on the starboard side (right side when sitting in the pilot’s seat), and a white light on the tail. This requirement is outlined in [Section 3.2.27.2].

question: What is a full emergency standby?
ground_truth: Full emergency standby means the level of response when an aircraft has, or is suspected to have, an operational defect that affects normal flight operations to the extent that there is possibility of an accident.
answer: A full emergency standby is the level of response when an aircraft has, or is suspected to have, an operational defect that affects normal flight operations to the extent that there is a possibility of an accident. This is outlined in Section 9.0 of the Canadian Aviation Regulations.

question: What is the minimum age for a small RPA basic operations pilot certificate?
ground_truth: A pilot certificate for small RPA (VLOS) basic operations is issued to those that are at least 14 years of age.
answer: The minimum age for a small RPA basic operations pilot certificate is 14 years old, as stated in Section 3.2.39.1.

question: What should a pilot do if they experience a communication failure?
ground_truth: The pilot should attempt to contact any ATC facility or another aircraft, inform them of the difficulty, and request they relay the information to the ATC facility with whom communications are intended.
answer: If a pilot experiences a communication failure, they should follow the procedures outlined in Section 6.3.2.1 of the TC AIM. Specifically, the pilot should select the transponder to reply to Mode A/3 Code 7600, maintain the appropriate altitude, and continue the flight along a route that would permit the aircraft to conduct a straight-in approach and landing without unexpected manoeuvring. The pilot should also attempt to establish communications using alternative methods if available, such as airborne telephone communications, CPDLC, or SATVOICE (as per Section 6.3.2.1).

question: What is the minimum visibility for VFR flight in uncontrolled airspace below 1,000 feet AGL during the day?
ground_truth: The minimum visibility is 2 statute miles during the day for aircraft other than helicopters. For helicopters, the minimum is 1 statute mile.
answer: The minimum visibility for VFR flight in uncontrolled airspace below 1,000 feet AGL during the day is not less than 2 miles (Section 2.7.2).

question: What is the minimum visibility for VFR flight in controlled airspace?
ground_truth: The minimum visibility for VFR flight in controlled airspace is 3 statute miles.
answer: The minimum visibility for VFR flight within a control zone is not less than 3 miles, as outlined in Table 2.2 of the TC AIM. This requirement is specified in CAR 602.114.

question: What is the purpose of anti-collision lights?
ground_truth: Anti-collision lights must be turned on to warn other aircraft. The pilot-in-command may turn them off if they determine that turning them off is in the interest of aviation safety.
answer: The purpose of anti-collision lights, such as strobe lights, landing lights, or recognition lights, is to aid in the detection and orientation of aircraft, especially during night operations. These lights make the aircraft more visible to other pilots and observers, enhancing safety by reducing the risk of collisions (Section 3.2.27.1).

question: What are the classifications of emergency conditions?
ground_truth: Emergency conditions are classified as: Distress — a condition of being threatened by serious and/or imminent danger and requiring immediate assistance; and Urgency — a condition concerning the safety of an aircraft or its occupants.
answer: The classifications of emergency conditions are distress and urgency as outlined in Section 4.1. Distress is a condition of being threatened by serious and/or imminent danger requiring immediate assistance, while urgency concerns the safety of an aircraft or persons on board but does not require immediate assistance.

question: What aircraft are exempt from carrying an emergency locator transmitter (ELT)?
ground_truth: Gliders, balloons, airships, and ultralight aeroplanes are exempt from carrying an ELT.
answer: Glider, balloons, airships, ultralight aeroplanes, aircraft operated by the holder of a flight training unit operating certificate engaged in flight training within 25 NM of the departure aerodrome, and additional exemptions as contained in CAR 605.38 are exempt from carrying an emergency locator transmitter (ELT) as per Section 4.1.

question: What is a control area extension?
ground_truth: Control area extensions are designated around aerodromes where the controlled airspace provided is insufficient to permit the required separation between IFR arrivals and departures and to contain IFR aircraft within controlled airspace.
answer: A control area extension is designated airspace around aerodromes to provide additional controlled airspace for IFR control, allowing for the required separation between IFR arrivals and departures and containing IFR aircraft within controlled airspace. Control area extensions are based at 2,200 ft AGL unless specified otherwise and extend up to, but not including, 18,000 ft ASL. They may be used to provide additional controlled airspace around busy aerodromes or to connect a control zone with the en route structure (Section 2.7.2).

question: Can an RPAS enter transponder-required airspace without a transponder?
ground_truth: ANSPs may allow an RPAS to enter transponder-required airspace without a transponder if the pilot requests permission prior to entering the area and aviation safety is not likely to be affected.
answer: An RPAS may be allowed to enter transponder-required airspace without a transponder if the pilot requests permission from the ANSP prior to entering the area and aviation safety is not likely to be affected (CAR 901.46(2)). However, unless permitted by the ANSP, all aircraft, including RPAS, flying in transponder-required airspace are required to have transponders (CAR 901.46(1)). Flexibility and patience are required as ANSPs may not be able to grant all requests to enter transponder airspace without a transponder (Section 3.2.34.2).

question: What is the passing mark for the PPL written examination?
ground_truth: The passing mark is 60%.
answer: The passing mark for the Private Pilot Licence—Aeroplane (PPL-A) written examination is 60% (Section 1.7.2).

question: What is the rule when two aircraft are approaching head-on?
ground_truth: When two aircraft are approaching head-on or approximately so, each pilot shall alter heading to the right.
answer: When two aircraft are approaching head-on or approximately so and there is a risk of collision, the pilot-in-command of each aircraft shall alter its heading to the right, as per Section 602.24(a).

question: What does the Canada Water Aerodrome Supplement (CWAS) contain?
ground_truth: The CWAS provides tabulated data and graphical information in support of Canadian VFR charts. It contains an aerodrome/facilities directory of all water aerodromes shown on Canadian VFR charts and lists communications station data, radio aids and other supplemental data.
answer: The Canada Water Aerodrome Supplement (CWAS) contains an aerodrome/facilities directory of all water aerodromes shown on Canadian VFR charts, lists communications station data, radio aids, and other supplemental data. This information is provided in support of Canadian VFR charts (Section 2.5.4).
